name: Search Autocomplete
mermaid_diagram:
graph TD
    Client(["Client Browser / Mobile App"]) -->|"1. Keystroke (100ms Debounce)"| Gateway["API Gateway"]
    Gateway -->|"2. Check Prefix Cache"| AppCache[("Application Cache - Redis")]
    Gateway -->|"3. Autocomplete Request"| AutoSvc["Autocomplete Service"]
    AutoSvc -->|"4. Query Hash Partition"| TrieCluster["Trie Server Cluster"]
    AutoSvc -->|"5. Fetch Trending Delta"| DeltaStore[("Trending Delta Store - Redis ZSet")]
    AutoSvc -->|"6. Merge & Rank Results"| AutoSvc
    Client -->|"7. Search Submit Event"| LogQueue["Log Streaming Queue - Kafka"]
    LogQueue -->|"8. Stream Aggregation"| StreamProc["Real-Time Analytics - Flink"]
    StreamProc -->|"9. Update Delta Rankings"| DeltaStore
    LogQueue -->|"10. Dump Raw Logs"| DataLake[("Data Lake - HDFS / S3")]
    DataLake -->|"11. Weekly Batch Index Build"| MapReduce["Batch Processing - Spark"]
    MapReduce -->|"12. Deploy Updated Trie Index"| TrieCluster

tradeoffs.option_b: Pre-computed Top-K Suggestions at Each Node
tradeoffs.chosen: Pre-computed Top-K Suggestions at Each Node
tradeoffs.reason: Dynamic sorting at query time requires traversing all leaf nodes under a matching prefix subtree and sorting candidate terms by frequency: for a single-character prefix like 'a' with V = 1,000,000 subtree items, an O(V log V) sort executes over 20 million comparison operations, consuming 200–500ms of CPU time per keystroke. Storing a pre-computed array of the top K=10 most frequent 64-bit query IDs directly inside each Trie node reduces read complexity to O(k) for a prefix of length k, bounding lookup latency to under 2ms (traversing k <= 10 nodes and reading the 10-element array). The cost is a memory overhead penalty: storing 10 pre-computed 64-bit IDs per node across 100 million Trie nodes adds 8 GB of RAM (10 x 8 bytes x 100M nodes = 8 GB), which is a favorable trade-off to eliminate expensive query-path subtree traversals.

Prefix-based sharding maps terms to servers by initial character, which creates severe load imbalances because single-letter prefixes like 'a' or 'e' receive up to 100x more query traffic than 'z' or 'q'. Hash-based sharding computes Hash(prefix) % N to distribute prefix lookup requests uniformly across an N-node cluster, keeping server CPU utilization balanced within a 5% margin. The trade-off is that single-node range queries (such as scanning all prefixes starting with 'app') can no longer be executed on a single host and require scatter-gather queries across partitions, which is acceptable because autocomplete operations are single-prefix lookups rather than range scans.

tradeoffs.option_b: Weekly Batch Trie Rebuild with Real-Time Delta Store
tradeoffs.chosen: Weekly Batch Trie Rebuild with Real-Time Delta Store
tradeoffs.reason: Mutating a distributed Trie synchronously on every user search query creates intense write lock contention across high-fanout prefix nodes, increasing write latency and risking pointer corruptions during high-concurrency traffic (100,000 queries/sec). An offline batch pipeline (using Spark/MapReduce) processes raw search logs weekly to build an immutable, highly compressed Trie index, while a real-time delta layer (Redis Sorted Sets) tracks breaking news trends over a rolling 1-hour window. The application server fetches the top 10 static Trie results and top 10 trending delta results, performing a 20-element min-heap merge in under 3ms at query time, accepting a brief 1-hour propagation delay for historical frequency counts in exchange for absolute write-path stability.

tradeoffs.option_b: Strict Exact Prefix Matching with Fallback Spellchecker
tradeoffs.chosen: Strict Exact Prefix Matching with Fallback Spellchecker
tradeoffs.reason: Supporting real-time fuzzy matching inside the primary Trie lookup path using naive unpruned branch exploration expands worst-case search complexity from O(k) to O(k * 26^d) for an alphabet of 26 characters. For a 5-character prefix (k=5) at edit distance d=1, naive exploration visits up to 130 branch paths, increasing average lookup latency from 2ms to over 45ms per keystroke. Restricting the primary autocomplete index to strict exact prefix matching guarantees a sub-5ms SLA across all keystrokes, while offloading typo correction to an isolated spellchecker service (or Levenshtein Automaton engine) that triggers only as a fallback when an exact prefix returns zero results.

interview_questions: In a prefix-sharded Trie cluster (e.g. Server 1 holds prefixes 'a'–'c', Server 2 holds 'd'–'f'), single-letter prefixes like 'a' receive 100 times more query traffic than 'z'. Describe two distinct strategies to distribute lookup load for single-letter and two-letter hot prefixes across a 50-node cluster without duplicating the entire Trie dataset on every node.

Each Trie node stores a pre-computed array of the top 10 most frequent search terms for that prefix. When a new query (e.g., 'breaking news event') suddenly spikes to 100,000 searches per minute, describe the exact bottom-up propagation algorithm and lock-free data structure update needed to refresh the top 10 arrays along the prefix path without causing read latency spikes on active Trie lookups.

A user types 'iphne' (1 edit distance from 'iphone'). Traversing the entire Trie with a Levenshtein edit distance of d=1 increases worst-case search space from O(k) to O(k * |Alphabet|^d), where k is prefix length and |Alphabet|=26. Explain how to bound fuzzy search latency to under 15ms using Levenshtein Automata or N-gram index lookups, and specify under what traffic conditions fuzzy matching should be bypassed entirely.

Your autocomplete architecture uses a weekly offline MapReduce batch build to generate the primary Trie, and a real-time stream processing pipeline (Kafka + Redis Sorted Sets) to track breaking news trends. Describe the exact read-path algorithm used by the application service to merge the static Trie results with the real-time trending delta layer within a 10ms SLA, and detail how you prevent stale batch results from suppressing sudden trending queries.

A Trie containing 100 million unique search terms with top 10 pre-computed suggestion strings consumes 30 GB of raw memory when implemented with standard pointer-based nodes (unordered_map<char, Node*>). Describe how to compact this Trie using a Radix Tree (Patricia Trie) or Succinct Double-Array Trie (DAT) layout to reduce memory footprint by 70% while maintaining O(k) lookup latency.

An autocomplete input field triggers an HTTP request per keystroke. Explain how client-side debouncing (e.g., 100ms threshold), browser local storage caching, and CDN edge caching (Cloudflare KV / Anycast) interact. If a user types 'system design' at 50 words per minute, calculate how many backend autocomplete RPCs reach your application servers versus being served from edge or client caches.

A search engine wants to personalize autocomplete suggestions (e.g., prioritizing 'python tutorial' for a software developer vs 'python snake species' for a biologist) while maintaining a global top-10 cache. How do you design a high-throughput read path that combines pre-computed global Trie suggestions with a user-specific context vector in under 5ms without running expensive real-time ML scoring on every keystroke?

A botnet submits millions of queries containing hate speech or offensive terms to artificially inflate their search frequencies and force them into top 10 pre-computed Trie nodes. How do you design a real-time query ingestion filter and Trie node update pipeline to sanitize, flag, and suppress malicious terms before they get pre-computed into public Trie nodes?
